primary.title: Pulmonary tuberculosis - a review of clinical features and diagnosis

secondary.title: Prevalence of anemia among patients with tuberculosis: systematic review and meta-analysis

source.selection_note: No single SR covering all clinical features of PTB exists. Primary source is a comprehensive NR (PMID:15535337) supplemented by topic-specific SRs and large cohorts.

edges.reason: Cough is the most prevalent respiratory symptom in PTB, driven by granulomatous inflammation and airway irritation

source_cpt.excerpt: cough 80% (28/35) in symptomatic cohort; range 23-90% across cohorts

edges.reason: Dyspnea indicates extensive parenchymal disease, large pleural effusion, or endobronchial TB
edges._note: state distribution (on_exertion vs at_rest) estimated — no primary data for split. NEEDS VERIFICATION

edges.reason: GI symptoms from swallowed sputum or cytokine-mediated vagal reflex
Occasional GI involvement via swallowed sputum or cytokine effect
Classic TB sign driven by diurnal cytokine level fluctuation peaking at night
TNF-α driven catabolic state — historically called 'consumption'
Pleural inflammation from peripheral Ghon focus involvement
Caseous granuloma eroding pulmonary vasculature or Rasmussen aneurysm rupture
TB characteristically has insidious onset over weeks to months

source_cpt.excerpt: symptoms insidiously progressive over weeks to months; 45.3% had >1yr of waxing/waning symptoms before diagnosis

edges._note: state distribution estimated from qualitative description. NEEDS VERIFICATION with quantitative data

edges.reason: TB onset is typically subacute to chronic

source_cpt.excerpt: insidious onset over weeks to months, often mistaken for allergies or routine fatigue

edges._note: state distribution estimated from qualitative description. NEEDS VERIFICATION with quantitative data

edges.reason: Fever frequency highly variable (18-100%) depending on cohort severity

source_cpt.excerpt: fever 18-42% (primary TB), 72.4% (118/421) in active cohort, 88% objective fever sign (28/32)

edges._note: temperature distribution across ranges estimated — no primary source for exact breakdown. NEEDS VERIFICATION

edges.reason: Lung crackles from consolidation; decreased breath sounds from effusion
Lymph node involvement from lymphatic spread of TB bacilli
Reticuloendothelial involvement in disseminated disease
Hepatic granulomatous involvement or reactive hepatitis
WBC can be elevated (neutrophilia) or depressed (miliary TB, immunosuppression)
edges._note: state distribution estimated from ranges. NEEDS VERIFICATION with exact cohort data

edges.reason: Acute phase reactant elevated in active TB reflecting systemic inflammation
edges._note: distribution across CRP ranges estimated. NEEDS VERIFICATION

edges.reason: CXR abnormal in 98.6% of symptomatic PTB; infiltrates in 99% of abnormal CXRs

source_cpt.excerpt: CXR abnormal 98.6%; infiltrates 99% of abnormal; cavity 66%; effusion 14-21.2%; hilar LAD 23%

edges._note: distribution across CXR categories estimated from multiple data points. NEEDS VERIFICATION

edges.reason: ESR almost universally elevated in active PTB due to hyperfibrinogenemia

source_cpt.excerpt: ESR elevated in 91.1-99.0% of newly diagnosed PTB patients

edges.reason: Hyponatremia from SIADH — ectopic ADH production by inflamed lung tissue
Negative acute phase protein + severe malnutrition/cachexia
edges._note: distribution between mildly_low and very_low estimated. NEEDS VERIFICATION

edges.reason: Anemia of chronic disease via IL-6/hepcidin pathway; iron sequestration in macrophages

source_cpt.excerpt: pooled anemia prevalence 61.53% (meta-analysis of 41 studies); mild 27.5-37.4%, moderate 27.5-52.5%, severe 6.2-10.1%

edges.reason: Normocytic normochromic (anemia of chronic disease) predominates; microcytic from iron deficiency

source_cpt.excerpt: normochromic normocytic 60.8-64.6% of anemia; hypochromic microcytic 27.8%

edges.reason: Reactive thrombocytosis from IL-6 driven thrombopoietin; thrombocytopenia in disseminated/miliary TB
AFB smear positivity at baseline varies 22.8-79% depending on cohort

source_cpt.excerpt: sputum AFB smear positive 79% (3916/5094) in large cohort; 22.8-68.2% in other cohorts

edges._note: Using 35% as conservative mid-range estimate for our general population model

edges.reason: Hepatic granulomatous involvement or reactive hepatitis

source_cpt.excerpt: transaminase (AST/ALT) elevation, ALP elevation reported in hepatic dissemination

edges._note: exact frequency not provided; estimated from clinical context. NEEDS VERIFICATION

data_quality_notes: No single SR covers all PTB clinical features — relied on NR (PMID:15535337) as primary + topic-specific SRs
Multiple edges have multi-state CPT distributions estimated from aggregate frequency — marked with NEEDS VERIFICATION
Frequency ranges are wide across cohorts due to population heterogeneity (HIV status, disease stage, geography)
T01/T02 temporal variables: CPT based on qualitative description, not n/N count
